text: 1 | P a g e
IN THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST
REPUBLIC OF SRI LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal filed in terms of
Section 331 of the Code of Criminal
Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979, read with
Article 138 of the Constitution of the
Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka.
Democratic Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka

1. Jayathilaka Dissanayaka Ariyapala
alias Heenmalli
2. Ranasinghe Arachchige Matin alias
Kehel Lokumahaththaya (deceased)
3. Hewa Halpage Jayasena alias Wijitha
4. Seeni Pallage Janaka

ayya

Katuwana Sudda

Prabath alias Premasiri Sudda
8. Wijenayaka Pathiranage Mahinda
9. Wadurapperuwage Premadasa alias
Nande Ayya
10. Ranasinghe Arachchige Jinadasa alias
Dingi Mama
Court of Appeal Case No:
High Court of Hambanthota Case No:
2 | P a g e
11. Gurusinghe Arachchige
Rathnadeshapriya
12. Ranasinghe Arachchige Chaminda
13. Hewa Halpage Wasantha
14. Wijenayaka Pathiranage Priyantha
alias Kumara
15. Jayawickrama Kankanamge Dilan
Manjula alias Kammale Sudda
16. Wijenataka Pathiranage Keerthi
17. Gurusinghe Arachchige Rukman alias
Shantha
18. Baragamage Nihal Nishantha alias
Gamunu
19. Ranasinghe Arachchige Prasad
Sanjeewa
20. Madurapperumage Ranjith
Pushpakumara
21. PahalaMalli Gedara Nawarathna alias
Janaka

AND NOW BETWEEN
1. Saman Bulathgama alias Kalu Aggala
ayya
2. Seeni Pallage Lalith Prasanna alias
Katuwana Sudda

alias Dingi Mama
4. Hewa Halpage Wasantha
5. Jayawickrama Kankanamge Dilan
Manjula alias Kammale Sudda
3 | P a g e
6. PahalaMalli Gedara Nawarathna alias
Janaka
Accused – Appellants Vs
Hon. Attorney General,
Attorney General’s Department,
Colombo 12.
Complainant - Respondent
Before : P. Kumararatnam, J.
Pradeep Hettiarachchi, J.
Counsel : Anil Silva, PC, with Anjana Abeyratne for the 6th Accused-Appellant
: Neranjan Jayasinghe with Randunu Heellage and Pravindika Kularathne
for the 21st Accused-Appellant.
: Shavindra Fernando, PC, with R.F. Dopulle for the 14th and 15th Accused-
Appellants
: D. Mithrapala with Chinthaka Rankothge for the 10th Accused-Appellant
: Shanaka Wijesinhe, A.S.G. for the Respondent.
Argued on : 25.05.2026
Decided on : 09.07.2026
4 | P a g e
Pradeep Hettiarachchi, J
Judgment
1. The case giving rise to this appeal was tried before the High Court of Hambantota by a
Judge sitting without a jury. The Attorney-General initially indicted 21 accused on
charges arising out of the death of Wijesingha Kankanamge Nandathissa and the
injuries caused to Wijesingha Kankanamge Jayatissa, Abewikrama Kankanamge
Emalihamy, and Wijesingha Kankanamge Ajith Priyantha. The indictment alleged that
the offences were committed by the accused as members of an unlawful assembly and,
in the alternative, in furtherance of a common intention.
2. At the conclusion of the trial, the Learned Trial Judge acquitted the remaining accused
of all charges. However, the 5th, 6th, 10th, 13th, 15th, and 21st accused were convicted on
the 1st and 2nd counts, namely, for being members of an unlawful assembly and
committing the murder of Wijesingha Kankanamge Nandatissa. In addition, the 5th and
6th accused were convicted under Section 314 of the Penal Code for causing hurt to
Abewikrama Kankanamge Emalihamy.
3. Being aggrieved by the said convictions and sentences, the 5th, 6th, 10th, 13th, 15th, and
21st accused (hereinafter referred to as "the Appellants") preferred the present appeal.
During the pendency of the appeal, the 5th Appellant died.
4. The grounds of appeal urged on behalf of the Accused-Appellants, several of which are
common to all, may be summarized as follows;
a. Prosecution witness No. 5 failed the test of credibility, and the Learned Trial
Judge has failed to appreciate the deficiencies of the testimony of the PW5 as it
failed the test of consistency as well;
b. Whether the prosecution has proved that the 21st Accused-Appellant was a
member of the unlawful assembly; and,
5 | P a g e
c. The Learned Trial Judge has failed to evaluate and give adequate reasons for
rejecting defence evidence.
5. A principal contention advanced on behalf of all the Appellants is that the evidence of
PW5 lacks credibility and fails to satisfy the test of consistency.
6. Accordingly, a careful analysis of PW5’s evidence, particularly with regard to its
credibility and consistency, is of paramount importance to the determination of the
issues arising in this appeal.
7. According to PW5’s evidence, the alleged incident occurred on 14.04.2003, which was
a Sinhala New Year day. On account of the New Year festivities, PW5 and his family
members were expecting the arrival of the deceased at their home. However, when the
deceased failed to arrive, PW5, accompanied by his two nephews, Ruwan and Ranjith,
proceeded on a motorbike in search of him towards Andarawewa. On their way, they
encountered the 6th, 8th, and 15th accused.
8. When they reached Ketanwewa Junction, Ruwan was attacked by the 6th Accused. At
that stage, the 1st Accused also arrived and assaulted them with clubs. Ranjith managed
to escape; however, PW5 fell to the ground and was unable to flee. PW5 was thereafter
assaulted, following which the assailants fled the scene.
9. After being assaulted, PW5 ran towards the tank known as Ketanwewa. While running
along the bund, he fainted and remained unconscious for more than half an hour.
10. Thereafter, upon regaining consciousness, he heard noise and, on looking towards the
road, observed the deceased approaching along the Gonnoruwa main road. As the
deceased reached the culvert, he was assaulted by the 2nd, 13th, and 21st accused. The
10th Accused was also present at the scene and shouted words to the effect that the
deceased should not be allowed to escape.
11. PW5 further stated that the 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 8th, 10th, 14th, 15th, and 16th accused were
also present at the scene armed with katty knives. But the 1st Accused was not there.
6 | P a g e
12. Moreover, PW5 testified that after the deceased had been assaulted by the 13th and 21st
accused, he fell to the ground. Thereafter, the other accused allegedly ran towards the
incident from the residence of the 16th Accused. PW5 further testified that, at that time,
the 5th Accused obtained a hoe from the 16th Accused’s residence and struck the
deceased on the head with it.
13. However, PW5 testified that upon returning home after the incident, he did not inform
anyone about what had transpired, as no one was present at the house at the material
time. Nevertheless, according to his own evidence, both his mother and sister had also
been assaulted during the incident and had subsequently been admitted to hospital for
treatment.
14. Further, PW5 testified that he, too, sustained injuries during the incident and was
admitted to the Hambantota Hospital, where he remained under medical treatment for
a period of two days.
15. A careful examination of the evidence of PW5 reveals several material discrepancies
and inconsistencies that cast serious doubt on the accuracy and reliability of his
testimony. These are set out below;
16. PW5 testified before the Court that he was the person riding the bicycle at the time of
the incident. However, this version is inconsistent with his statement to the Police,
wherein he stated that it was his nephew, Sampath (PW7), who rode the bicycle.
“බයිසිකලය පැදගෙන අගේ සම්පත් පුතා” (5D1, marked in evidence at vide page
266 of the Appeal Brief)
17. Furthermore, in his statement to the Police, PW5 stated that he traveled at the rear of
the group on the motorcycle. This statement is inconsistent with his testimony before
the Court that he was riding the bicycle, thereby creating a material discrepancy
regarding his position and movements at the relevant time. “මම ෙමන් කගේ යතුරු
පැදිගේ පිටුපසින්මයි.” (5D2, marked in evidence at vide page 266 of the Appeal
Brief)
7 | P a g e
18. PW5 also gave inconsistent accounts regarding the time at which he went in search of
the deceased. In his examination-in-chief, he testified that he went in search of the
deceased at approximately 2.45 p.m. During cross-examination, however, he stated for
the first time that he left the residence in search of the deceased at about 4.00-4.30 p.m.
This version was not disclosed previously. Moreover, in his statement to the police,
PW5 stated that he went in search of his brother, the deceased, at about 12,45 p.m.
These materially divergent accounts regarding the timing of the events further
undermine the credibility and reliability of PW’5 evidence. “අයියා අගේ නැති නිසා
අන්දරවැව යන්න ගියා හවස 12.45 පමණ බයිසිකලගයන් ගියා.” (5D3, marked
in evidence at vide page 267 of the Appeal Brief)
19. Further, the defence has established a material omission in the evidence of PW5. In his
statement to the police as well as in the proceedings before the Magistrate’s Court, PW5
did not make any reference to the 5th Accused-Appellant assaulting the deceased on the
head with a hoe. The said material fact was introduced for the first time in his testimony
before the trial court, thereby giving rise to a significant omission affecting the
consistency of his version.
20. Moreover, PW5 identified the 21st Appellant as Kumara; during his testimony.
However, it is pertinent to note that the actual name of the 21st Accused is not Kumara.
This misidentification further casts doubt on the accuracy of PW5’s identification
evidence.
“ප්‍ර: දැන් තමුන් කිේවා 2,13, 21 විත්තිකරුවන් අයියාට ෙැහුවා කියලා?
උ: ඔේ
ප්‍ර: ඔවුන් නමින් දන්නවද?
උ: කුමාර කියලා තමයි දන්ගන්.” (at vide page 248 of the Appeal Brief)
21. The next witness called on behalf of the prosecution was PW2, the sister of the
deceased. According to her evidence, on the day of the incident, she visited her mother’s
residence. Thereafter, PW5 and two of her sister's sons proceeded in search of the
deceased to Andarawewa on a bicycle. She further testified that, after a short while,
PW5 returned in a state of distress and informed her that they had been assaulted.
8 | P a g e
“උ: ටික ගවලාවකින් මේි දුවලා ඇවිේලා අපිට ෙහනවා කිේවා. අක්කගේ
පුතාලා ගදන්නටත් ෙහනවා කිේවා.
ප්‍ර: තමන්ට මේි ගකගනක් කිේගේ ෙහනවා කියලා තමන් සදහන් කගේ?
උ: ඔේ.
ප්‍ර: එගහම කේද ඇවිේලා කිේගේ?
උ: අජිත් ප්‍රියන්ත.” (at vide page 286 of the Appeal Brief)
22. Accordingly, PW2, along with PW4, PW3, the wife of PW3, and the husband of PW2,
proceeded to the scene of the incident. She further testified that, by that time, the
deceased had already fallen to the ground after being assaulted.
23. Further, she testified that the 6th and 15th accused continued to assault the deceased
while he was lying on the ground in a defenceless state.
“ප්‍ර: තමන් නන්දතිස්සට ෙහලා වැටිලා හිටිය කිේවා. කවුද නන්දතිස්සට
උ: කටුවන සුද්දා, කම්මගේ සුද්දා වැටිලා ඉන්නගකාට ෙහනවා දැක්ගක්.
කැත්ගතන් ෙහනවා දැක්ගක් නැහැ. කටුවන සුද්දායි, කම්මගේ සුද්දායි ඒ
ගදන්න තමයි ෙැහුගේ.” (at vide page 288 of the Appeal Brief)
24. When PW4 was attempting to protect PW3 (Jayatissa), the 6th and 15th Accused-
Appellants allegedly attacked her with a katty. PW2 further stated that at that time the
3rd, 4th, 5th, 9th, 10th, 12th, 13th, and 18th accused were also present at the scene.
25. The defence elicited several material contradictions and omissions in the evidence of
PW2 as follows;
26. According to her testimony, the family became aware of the alleged incident only after
PW5 informed them of what had transpired. However, this version is clearly
inconsistent with the evidence of PW5, who stated that upon returning home after the
incident, no one was present at the residence and, as such, he was unable to inform
9 | P a g e
anyone of the occurrence. These mutually contradictory versions create doubt about the
reliability and coherence of the prosecution evidence in that regard.
27. Further, the defence marked several material omissions in the evidence of PW2;
whereas, PW2 did not disclose in her statement to the police that PW4 had been attacked
by the 6th and 15th Accused-Appellants.
28. The next witness called on behalf of the prosecution was PW4, the mother of the
deceased. According to her testimony, PW5 and her two grandsons had proceeded to
Andarawewa in search of the deceased due to his absence on the day of the incident.
Thereafter, upon hearing a commotion, she proceeded to the scene of the crime together
with PW2, PW3, and her daughter-in-law. Upon arriving at the scene, she witnessed
that the 10th Accused was allegedly assaulting PW3. She further testified that she was
attacked by the 5th, 6th, and 15th Accused-Appellants with clubs and katty knife, causing
injuries to her hand and back. According to her evidence, she subsequently lost
consciousness. Therefore, it is evident that PW4 did not witness the actual assault on
the deceased.
29. It is noteworthy that, while PW2 testified that they proceeded to the scene of the
incident after PW5 informed them of the alleged assault, PW4 gave a materially
different account. According to PW4, they proceeded to the scene upon hearing a
commotion emanating from the area. These two versions are plainly inconsistent as to
how they became aware of the incident and what prompted them to go to the scene.
This contradiction casts further doubt on the reliability of the prosecution witnesses and
undermines the consistency of the prosecution narrative regarding the sequence of
events leading up to their arrival at the scene of the incident.
“ප්‍ර: ඒ අය ගෙදරින් ගිගේ කීයට විතරද?
ප්‍ර: ඒ ගියාට පස්ගස් ගමාකද වුගන්?
උ: ගියාට පස්ගස් පර පැත්ගත් විලාපයක් ඇහුනා.” (at vide page 319 of the
Appeal Brief)
10 | P a g e
30. PW3, the brother of the deceased, testified that he was also present in relation to the
incident and gave evidence as to what allegedly transpired. According to his evidence,
he and his wife had proceeded to water their cattle. While so proceeding, they heard
commotion, following which he was allegedly subjected to abusive language by the
10th Accused-Appellant, and thereafter, attacked by him with a club.
31. Further, he testified that the 6th, 8th, 9th, 11th, 14th, 15th, 16th, 17th accused were present
at the material time. However, it is significant that PW3 himself did not witness the
actual assault on the deceased.
32. The next witness called on behalf of the prosecution was PW7, the nephew of the
deceased. He testified that he, his brother, and PW5 proceeded from his grandmother’s
residence to Andarawewa in search of the deceased on a motorcycle, which was ridden
by him. According to his evidence, they were allegedly assaulted by four accused,
namely the 1st, 6th, 8th, and 15th Accused. He further stated that the 15th Accused-
Appellant assaulted him on the head. PW7 also stated that, upon being assaulted, PW5
fled from the scene, and thereafter he too fled together with his brother.
33. The version of events narrated by PW7 is inconsistent with the evidence of PW5.
According to PW5’s testimony, he was the person riding the motorcycle while
accompanied by his nephews. However, PW7’s evidence directly contradicts this
position, as he stated that it was he, PW7, who was riding the motorcycle at the material
time. This material discrepancy between the two witnesses gives rise to a significant
inconsistency in the prosecution evidence regarding a crucial aspect of the incident.
“ප්‍ර: අන්දරවැව ප්‍රගද්ශයට බයික් එගකන් තමාලා එනගකාට කවුද
පැදගෙන ගිගේ?
උ: මම ස්වාමිනි.
ප්‍ර: තමාද බයික් එක පැදගෙන ගිගේ?
උ: ඔේ ස්වාමිනි.” (at vide page 397 of the Appeal Brief)
34. Further, it is evident from the evidence of PW7 that PW5 fled from the scene
immediately after the alleged assault commenced. In these circumstances, a serious
11 | P a g e
doubt arises as to whether PW5 was in fact in a position to witness the entirety of the
incident as narrated in his evidence. This material aspect casts further doubt on the
reliability and veracity of PW5’s testimony, particularly as to his presence and ability
to observe the events he claims to have seen.
“ප්‍ර: මාමා පැනලා ගියාද?
උ: ඔේ.
ප්‍ර: මාමා ගකාගහද ගිගේ?
උ: දැක්ගක් නෑ.” (at vide page 402 of the Appeal Brief)
35. Accordingly, it is significant that PW5 lacks credibility and his evidence fails to satisfy
the test of consistency when compared with the testimonies of other prosecution
witnesses. The material contradictions and omissions revealed in his evidence, when
read in conjunction with the inconsistencies between his version and that of PW2, PW7,
and other witnesses, seriously undermine the reliability of his account.
36. E.R.S.R. Coomaraswamy, in his work ‘The Law of Evidence’, has described how a
Court should evaluate contradictions and omissions;
“ …. Another test that is applied in the evaluation of evidence is the test of
inconsistency, contradictions per se and discrepancies in the evidence of the
witness. A witness is often contradicted by his statements to the police under section
110 of the Code, 5 or by his depositions in the magistrate’s court. Some may be
positive contradictions, while others may be omissions, which may be of material
facts or immaterial facts. In evaluating discrepancies, contradictions and
omissions, it is undesirable for a court to pick out sentences and consider them in
isolation from the rest of the statements. The entire statement should be taken into
consideration to ascertain whether they are due to a deliberate attempt to suppress
or depart from the truth. Witnesses should not be disbelieved on the basis of trifling
discrepancies and omissions”
37. Further, it is noteworthy that a serious doubt arises as to whether PW5, who is projected
by the prosecution as the sole eyewitness to the incident, was in fact in a position to
12 | P a g e
witness the events as he has narrated in his evidence. If PW5 was not present throughout
the material time, the safety of relying upon his testimony to sustain a conviction against
the Appellants is gravely undermined. In such circumstances, his evidence cannot be
treated as wholly reliable or sufficient, and it would be unsafe to found convictions
against the Appellants solely or substantially upon his testimony in the absence of
independent corroboration.
38. To elaborate further, where material witnesses give inconsistent evidence on material
particulars, their testimony loses its probative value and cannot safely be relied upon.
Such inconsistencies inevitably cast serious doubt on the credibility of the prosecution
case.
39. Further, it was contended that the prosecution has failed to establish, beyond reasonable
doubt, that the 21st Accused-Appellant was a member of the alleged unlawful assembly.
40. In this regard, it is significant that only PW5 made reference to the presence of the 21st
Accused-Appellant and attributed to him participation in the assault on the deceased.
However, none of the other prosecution witnesses, namely PW2, PW4, and PW7, have
made any reference whatsoever to the presence or involvement of the 21st Accused-
Appellant at the material time. This account creates a serious lacuna in the prosecution
case against the 21st Accused-Appellant.
41. Moreover, as stated earlier in this judgment, PW5, during his testimony, identified the
21st Accused-Appellant as ‘Kumara.’ However, it is pertinent to note that the actual
name of the 21st Accused-Appellant is not ‘Kumara.’ This discrepancy raises a serious
question regarding the correctness of PW5’s identification of the 21st Accused-
Appellant and further undermines the reliability of his evidence on this crucial issue.
42. Material discrepancies in a witness's evidence concerning the identification of an
accused strike at the very root of the reliability of that evidence. In such circumstances,
it would be unsafe for the Court to act upon the testimony of that witness without
satisfactory corroboration.
13 | P a g e
43. In Sucha Singh Vs. State of Punjab, (2003) 7 SCC 643, it was held inter alia:
“.......normal discrepancies in evidence are those which are due to normal errors of
observation, normal errors of memory due to lapse of time, due to mental
disposition such as shock and horror at the time of occurrence and those are always
there however honest and truthful a witness may be. Material discrepancies are
those which are not normal, and not expected of a normal person. Courts have to
label the category to which a discrepancy may be categorized. While normal
discrepancies do not corrode the credibility of a party's case, material discrepancies
do so......Where it is not feasible to separate truth from falsehood, because grain
and chaff are inextricably mixed up, and in the process of separation an absolutely
new case has to be reconstructed by divorcing essential details presented by the
prosecution completely from the context and the background against which they are
made, the only available course to be made is to discard the evidence in toto.”
44. Further, since there exists a serious doubt as to whether PW5 actually witnessed the
assault on the deceased in the manner he has described, his testimony regarding the
presence of the 21st Accused-Appellant at the material time becomes highly doubtful
and unsafe to rely upon. In such circumstances, the evidence of PW5, being
uncorroborated on this crucial aspect and suffering from the issues of credibility and
reliability, cannot be accepted as a safe basis to sustain a finding against the 21st
Accused-Appellant. Therefore, it is my view that such identification cannot be regarded
as sufficient to establish, beyond reasonable doubt, the presence or participation of the
21st Accused-Appellant in the alleged unlawful assembly.
45. Additionally, it is noteworthy that the Learned Trial Judge failed to specify whether the
convictions of the 5th and 6th accused for voluntarily causing hurt were entered under
Count 4, which was based on unlawful assembly, or under Count 8, which was founded
on common intention. The judgment is silent on the precise count under which those
convictions were recorded, thereby giving rise to uncertainty as to the legal basis of the
convictions. In the absence of clear findings in this regard, the convictions are rendered
legally uncertain and cannot be sustained without proper judicial reasoning on the
specific foundation of liability.
14 | P a g e
46. The irrationality of the learned High Court Judge's reasoning is further manifested by
the acquittal of the 14th and 16th accused, notwithstanding the evidence that they too
were present at the scene armed with clubs and knives. The Appellants in the present
appeal were convicted on the basis that they were members of an unlawful assembly. If
the evidence accepted by the Learned High Court Judge established that the 14th and
16th accused were likewise present at the scene armed with clubs and knives, it is
difficult to comprehend the basis upon which they were acquitted. The judgment
discloses no cogent or plausible reasons for treating the 14th and 16th accused differently
from the Appellants, thereby revealing a material inconsistency in the evaluation of the
evidence and the conclusions reached by the Learned High Court Judge.
47. Upon careful consideration of the totality of the evidence and the material infirmities
discussed above, it is my considered view that the convictions of the Accused-
Appellants are unsafe and unsustainable in law. Accordingly, the convictions and
sentences imposed on the Accused-Appellants are set aside, and the Accused-
Appellants are acquitted of all charges.
48. The Appeal is accordingly allowed.
Judge of the Court of Appeal
P. Kumararatnam, J.
I agree
Judge of the Court of Appeal
